original_abstract: Policy advisory systems, which provide evidence-informed policy advice to governments, are put under great pressure in times of crisis. Crisis situations may engender conflicting interpretations by stakeholders about how to respond. These conflicts are compounded by inevitable gaps in knowledge and uncertainties about how the crisis might develop. All governments face challenges beyond their control which can take many forms, ranging from financial and political disruptions through to health pandemics, natural disasters, and threats to national security. Such decision-making pressures were especially evident during the COVID-19 crisis and followed different trajectories in different countries. This introductory article frames a group of research studies that brings new insights into various aspects of how governmental policy advisory systems responded to this crisis. Policy advice was formulated against a backdrop of controversies about how to prioritise health outcomes, economic livelihoods and social interaction. These studies analyse policy advisory systems with particular attention to the quality of the available knowledge base, the disciplinary mix of expertise within advisory bodies, the roles of experts as either trusted insiders or as external commentators, as providers or ‘shapers’ of knowledge, and the degree of (in)formality in their relationships with politicians. These issues are also investigated in the context of creeping crises, such as climate change. Taken together, the special issue provides new insights into how knowledge provided through policy advisory systems informs crisis governance. In this way, it advances our knowledge of the relationship between experts in policy advisory systems and political decision-makers in times of crisis.
humanized_abstract: Policy advisory systems bear a heavy burden when it comes to providing ministers with reliable policy advice in periods of crisis. These crises are often accompanied by political struggles between different actors about which course of action should be taken. There will always be gaps in knowledge about particular issues and the potential course of events that can be exploited in policy debates. But sometimes governments must react to events that are beyond their control: economic shocks, political disturbances, the occurrence of an epidemic, natural disasters or the threat of war and terrorism. All of these types of events came to the surface during the COVID-19 crisis and each country reacted differently. In this introductory paper to this themed section we present a collection of papers dealing with various aspects of policy advisory systems during periods of crisis, using the COVID-19 pandemic as a prime example. The papers delve into a number of issues faced by policy advisory systems: - the knowledge-based policy advice that had to be produced under huge pressure for government ministers to decide which course of action they would choose to tackle an urgent national health or economic emergency by balancing competing policy considerations such as health protection, economic stability and meeting the demands for people to connect and be with each other; - the importance of the composition of advice teams including which knowledge disciplines and with which types of advisers – in-house specialists or external independent experts with particular networks – to provide necessary counsel; - the particular roles that can be played by experts, e.g. whether these are longstanding advisors to government ministers whom they know very well or external specialists and accordingly whether experts ‘prick the surface’ or are so-called ‘shapers’ of policy; and - the many formats and degree of formality that policy advisory systems may enter into with politicians when devising responses to events such as these. These themes and the research they enabled are also relevant for governance of what have been called “creeping crises” – such as climate change. The themed issue makes a contribution to a better understanding of the role of experts in policy advisory systems and the way they in practice shape, influence or maybe in some cases have only a marginal influence on the policy choices that fall to government ministers – all against a backdrop of extraordinary and urgent pressure in periods of crisis.
